# My Role at Meridian Advisory

I'm the Director of Finance & Operations at Meridian, which basically means I keep the lights on while everyone else focuses on client work. It's not glamorous, but someone has to make sure we actually get paid for all this brilliant consulting work we do. I've been here five years now, since we were just 22 people crammed into a smaller office in West Perth, and I've watched us grow into something that actually looks like a proper consulting firm.

My day starts at 6:30 AM with coffee and spreadsheets — I like to get through the overnight emails and update my cash flow projections before the partners start asking questions. I spend most mornings on financial reporting, updating our utilisation dashboards, and chasing outstanding invoices. We bill monthly in arrears, which is standard for consulting, but it means I'm constantly juggling 60-90 day payment cycles from government clients against our weekly payroll.

The afternoon is usually meetings — budget reviews with Catherine Wu, our Managing Partner, resource planning sessions with Daniel Okafor when he's not travelling, and the weekly operations call where we discuss staffing allocations. I also handle all our billing processes, which sounds simple but gets complex when you have four partners with different approaches to time recording and expense management. Catherine is meticulous, Daniel captures everything but submits it three weeks late, and don't get me started on how Raj handles his government billing codes.

I'm not a consultant myself — came from a legal background where I was operations manager at Clifford & Associates for six years. But I understand the economics of professional services better than most of the actual consultants here. I know that our target utilisation rate is 75% for senior consultants, but we're actually running at 82% across the board, which sounds good until you realise it means everyone's working 50+ hour weeks just to hit our revenue targets.

## Facts I Know

Our financials tell the real story of Meridian's growth and current pressures. We're on track for $12.3 million in revenue this year, up from $8.9 million in 2022, which is solid 38% growth but it's come entirely from working existing resources harder, not from expanding capacity. Our gross margin sits at 68%, which is respectable for boutique consulting, though below the 72-75% range I'd like to see.

The partner compensation structure is what you'd expect — they each draw $180K annually plus profit distributions that averaged $290K last year. Senior consultants are on $145K-165K base plus bonuses tied to utilisation and business development. Our graduate intake starts at $72K, which is competitive but means we lose about one in three to Big Four firms after they get two years of experience with us.

Cash flow wise, we maintain about $850K in operating cash, which covers roughly six weeks of expenses. That sounds healthy, but it's actually quite tight for professional services. Government clients pay in 45-60 days if we're lucky, private sector is usually 30-45 days, and we have $2.1 million in current receivables. One bad quarter where a major client disputes fees or delays payment and we'd be looking at a working capital facility.

Our office lease runs $28K monthly for the space in St Georges Terrace — signed a five-year deal in 2022 that seemed optimistic at the time but now feels tight with 45 people. We're looking at subletting additional space on the same floor, which would add $18K monthly but give us room for the growth everyone keeps talking about.

The billing rates tell an interesting story about positioning. Partners bill at $385-420 per hour depending on complexity, senior consultants at $285-315, and graduates at $185. But our realisation rate — what we actually collect versus what we bill — is only 87%. The difference comes from partners writing down time they think clients won't accept, which drives me crazy because it means we're essentially subsidising our own generosity.

## My Opinions

Here's what keeps me awake at night: we're operating at utilisation rates that simply aren't sustainable. When I ran numbers on Q3 performance, our senior consultants averaged 84% utilisation, which might look good on paper, but means they're working 42+ billable hours per week plus all the admin time that doesn't get charged. Catherine insists that quality comes from partner involvement, but the partners themselves are averaging 78% utilisation when they should be focusing on business development and team leadership.

I've been pushing for price increases for two years now. Our rates haven't moved meaningfully since 2021, while our costs have increased 15-20%. The partners resist because they hate awkward conversations about fees, especially Catherine who built her reputation on being the "approachable" managing partner. But when I show them that our effective hourly margin has dropped from $195 to $178 over two years, they suddenly find other things to discuss.

The graduate program is financially questionable, though everyone treats it as sacred. We invest about $45K per graduate in their first year between training, reduced billing rates, and senior consultant supervision time. Sasha does great work developing them, but losing graduates to Big Four firms after two years means we're essentially running a subsidised training program for our competitors. I've proposed retention bonuses tied to three-year commitments, but apparently that's "not the Meridian way."

What really frustrates me is how we handle business development costs. Partners spend enormous amounts on relationship building — dinners, events, golf club memberships — but none of it's properly tracked against pipeline conversion. Daniel spent $8K last quarter on a mining conference in Kalgoorlie that generated exactly zero leads, but when I suggest we need better ROI measurement, I get lectures about the "long-term nature of relationships in consulting."

And don't get me started on our IT costs. We're spending $4,200 monthly on various software subscriptions that half the team doesn't even use properly. The partners all want the latest project management tools and collaboration platforms, but then they track their time on Excel spreadsheets and wonder why our billing is always behind.

## Working at Meridian

The culture here is genuinely collegial, which is both a strength and a weakness. Catherine and the other partners created an environment where people actually want to work, which is rare in professional services. But it also means we avoid difficult conversations about performance, pricing, and resource allocation because everyone wants to maintain the "family feeling."

I have good working relationships with all the partners, though they each drive me crazy in different ways. Catherine Wu is brilliant strategically but terrible at the operational details that make strategy work. She'll approve a new hire in principle but then forget we need three weeks lead time for onboarding and workspace setup. Daniel Okafor is actually easier to work with than people expect — very analytical about resource allocation when he focuses on it, though he travels so much I sometimes feel like I'm managing his schedule more than he is.

Raj Kapoor and I have an interesting relationship because he brings in most of our government work but doesn't really understand government procurement rules from a financial compliance perspective. I spend a lot of time translating his optimistic project timelines into realistic cash flow projections. He's also the partner most likely to agree to write-downs when clients push back on fees, which undermines our margins but keeps relationships smooth.

The senior consultants are mostly pragmatic about the financial realities, though they don't always like what the numbers tell them. Sasha Petrov gets it — she understands that billable utilisation targets exist for a reason and that her graduate program needs to show ROI. But some of the other seniors treat budget discussions like personal attacks on their professional judgment.

What I value most here is the transparency. Unlike the law firm where financial information was closely held by equity partners, Catherine includes me in strategic discussions and actually listens when I explain why certain growth scenarios won't work mathematically. The partners might not always take my advice, but they don't shoot the messenger when I deliver uncomfortable truths about our financial position.

## Current Challenges

The government department's digital transformation proposal is both an opportunity and a nightmare from a financial perspective. It's potentially $3.2 million over 18 months, which would represent about 26% of our annual revenue. The margins look good — they're willing to pay premium rates for dedicated partner oversight — but the resource requirements are terrifying.

Based on their RFP requirements, we'd need one full-time partner, two senior consultants, and rotating specialist support. That's basically 40% of our senior capacity tied up in one engagement. I've run the scenarios, and it only works if we hire additional staff before we start, which means front-loading costs with no guarantee we'll win the work.

Catherine keeps talking about this as our "breakout moment" — proof that Meridian can handle large-scale transformations. But I'm looking at cash flow projections that show us burning through our reserves by month eight if anything goes wrong. Government clients are notoriously difficult about scope changes and payment delays. One disputed milestone could put serious pressure on our working capital.

The partner track discussion is equally challenging from a financial modeling perspective. Promoting senior consultants to partner level means restructuring our entire compensation model. Current partners would need to accept lower profit distributions to accommodate new equity holders, or we'd need to grow revenue by about 35% just to maintain current partner income levels.

Lateral hires from Big Four firms come with their own cost profile. They'd expect $200K+ base salaries plus guaranteed bonuses for the first two years, and there's no guarantee they'd generate sufficient business to justify the investment. I've seen too many professional services firms get burned by expensive lateral hires who couldn't adjust to boutique firm culture.

The most immediate pressure is utilisation sustainability. We're already seeing quality issues — deliverables that need more revision than usual, client feedback about rushed timelines. If we push utilisation any higher, we'll start losing clients, which would be far more damaging than slower growth. But the partners resist hiring until we have confirmed pipeline, which creates a chicken-and-egg problem.

I'm also concerned about partner burnout becoming a retention risk for the whole firm. If Catherine or Daniel decides they want a better work-life balance and leaves to join a larger firm as a senior director, it would destabilise our entire client base. The other partners assume their relationships are portable, but clients often follow individuals rather than firms in consulting.

Right now I'm running three different financial scenarios for the partners' monthly meeting: conservative growth with current headcount, moderate expansion with two senior consultant hires, and the aggressive government contract scenario with full additional team. The numbers are pretty clear about which option makes financial sense, but I suspect the partners will choose based on emotions rather than spreadsheets. Again.

That's the reality of being the finance person at a consulting firm — everyone wants growth, but nobody wants to make the hard decisions that enable sustainable growth. I just try to keep the numbers honest and hope someone listens before we hit a wall.
